Hi. I am a community member, working on Stage 0 proposals JSON.parseBinary and ArrayBuffer.prototype.detach.

Matteo Collina at Fastify has given positive feedback, Daniel Minor at V8 suggested I bring them here to find a potential champion, readme on GitHub of TC39 mentions this specific Matrix channel. 
Happy to answer questions and end one month streak of stage 0.

Use-cases: receiving payload by servers (not only http), receiving payload by clients, jsonwebtokens, filesystem manipulations, memory-constrained environments, high-throughput network communication. Most of the applications out there.

Links to the repositories with Open-Source benchmarks and examples: 
https://github.com/Guthib-of-Dan/proposal-json-parse-binary 
https://github.com/Guthib-of-Dan/proposal-arraybuffer-detach
content.formatted_body:
Hi. I am a community member, working on Stage 0 proposals JSON.parseBinary and ArrayBuffer.prototype.detach.

Matteo Collina at Fastify has given positive feedback, Daniel Minor at V8 suggested I bring them here to find a potential champion, readme on GitHub of TC39 mentions this specific Matrix channel.
Happy to answer questions and end one month streak of stage 0.

Use-cases: receiving payload by servers (not only http), receiving payload by clients, jsonwebtokens, filesystem manipulations, memory-constrained environments, high-throughput network communication. Most of the applications out there.

Links to the repositories with Open-Source benchmarks and examples:
https://github.com/Guthib-of-Dan/proposal-json-parse-binary
https://github.com/Guthib-of-Dan/proposal-arraybuffer-detach

content.body: On json-parse-binary, I find it surprising that the explainer does not appear to make any reference to the `{type: 'json'}` import attribute, or Fetch's `Response.json()` method. Is that intentional?
content.formatted_body: On json-parse-binary, I find it surprising that the explainer does not appear to make any reference to the {type: 'json'} import attribute, or Fetch's Response.json() method. Is that intentional?

content.body: I have a hard time understanding what problem json-parse-binary is solving. The only times I ever have a byte array containing JSON are when reading from the network or disk. From the network there's already `.json()`, and from disk there's `type: 'json'` imports. So... what's the remaining use case?
content.formatted_body: I have a hard time understanding what problem json-parse-binary is solving. The only times I ever have a byte array containing JSON are when reading from the network or disk. From the network there's already .json(), and from disk there's type: 'json' imports. So... what's the remaining use case?

content.body: for `.detach`, I think if you are in a situation where you are allocating and detaching a bunch of arraybuffers, something has already gone wrong. You'd generally be doing `encodeInto` or `setFromBase64` or similar, to avoid allocating the arraybuffers in the first place. So it is difficult for me to imagine a situation where the overhead of `.transfer(0)` is relevant
content.formatted_body: for .detach, I think if you are in a situation where you are allocating and detaching a bunch of arraybuffers, something has already gone wrong. You'd generally be doing encodeInto or setFromBase64 or similar, to avoid allocating the arraybuffers in the first place. So it is difficult for me to imagine a situation where the overhead of .transfer(0) is relevant

content.body:
There is nothing to imagine, but see the benchmarks. Literally speed boost.

I didn’t mention imports or Response.json, because that might look as rude that I want to change something, before I even found a delegate. But yes, asking WhatWG to use JSON.parseBinary in Fetch and also making use of it in json imports were one of my subsequent ideas, just not listed. So yeah, intentionally.
content.formatted_body:
There is nothing to imagine, but see the benchmarks. Literally speed boost.

I didn’t mention imports or Response.json, because that might look as rude that I want to change something, before I even found a delegate. But yes, asking WhatWG to use JSON.parseBinary in Fetch and also making use of it in json imports were one of my subsequent ideas, just not listed. So yeah, intentionally.

content.body: Microbenchmarks are almost never convincing without a story for why a real application might do something anything like as frequently as a microbenchmark
Browsers could already implement `.json` and `type: "json"` imports as parsing over binary; I don't think they've done so but there's nothing stopping them
content.formatted_body: Browsers could already implement .json and type: "json" imports as parsing over binary; I don't think they've done so but there's nothing stopping them

content.body: Saying that “it saves cpu time and memory” doesn’t convince you?
Not on its own, no.

Basically any possible standard library functions would save nonzero cpu and memory for nonzero programs. There needs to be more than that, such as an argument that it would save significant cpu and memory for many programs.

Parsing over binary json saves us from constant reallocations + UTF16 upgrade of a string.
By avoiding Error we potentially hide stack trace from unintentional leakages.
If browser had implemented this, it would be even easier to expose such JSON parser, so nothing stops them, and nothing stops us.
content.formatted_body:
Parsing over binary json saves us from constant reallocations + UTF16 upgrade of a string.
By avoiding Error we potentially hide stack trace from unintentional leakages.
If browser had implemented this, it would be even easier to expose such JSON parser, so nothing stops them, and nothing stops us.

content.body: What I mean is: if your argument is "reading json from the network is slow because it goes through a string", then the solution is to convince browsers to implement a faster `.json()`, not to add a feature to the language
content.formatted_body: What I mean is: if your argument is "reading json from the network is slow because it goes through a string", then the solution is to convince browsers to implement a faster .json(), not to add a feature to the language

content.body: .json() belongs to Response, but in the case of node:http, we receive payload as chunks and not as a Response object

For things to be worth adding to the language, they need to at minimum be useful in browsers, not just in node. but fwiw node already vendors simdjson so they might be open to exposing it to userland if you ask.

Concerning the “detach” and avoiding allocating ArrayBuffer, in the same node:http these chunks are allocated by the framework, not us. By using “detach” we would  optimise our handlers. If not everyone, then libraries we use every dat can adopt it.
I would think an additional ArrayBuffer allocation is ~never going to be a relevant amount of CPU time when the network is involved, but I could be wrong about that.
* I would think an additional zero-length ArrayBuffer allocation is ~never going to be a relevant amount of CPU time when the network is involved, but I could be wrong about that.

m.new_content.body: I would think an additional zero-length ArrayBuffer allocation is ~never going to be a relevant amount of CPU time when the network is involved, but I could be wrong about that.

content.body: Not only node, but Bun, Deno as well. I am proposing this to a language because there is a wide choice of instruments.
* Not only Node, but Bun, Deno as well. I am proposing this to a language because there is a wide choice of instruments.

m.new_content.body: Not only Node, but Bun, Deno as well. I am proposing this to a language because there is a wide choice of instruments.

content.body: WinterTC is the appropriate venue for server-runtime-only features
Although IIRC bun and deno do expose `Response`-based HTTP servers, so they could just use `.json` already
content.formatted_body: Although IIRC bun and deno do expose Response-based HTTP servers, so they could just use .json already

content.body: By using Response.json we restrain ourselves from handling either network error, or parsing error. One method throws for both cases
also wait node's request does have `.json`

content.body: So it can adopt these language features. Or I should spend much more time asking each of them, while there is a centralised place like ECMAScript specification?
`.json` already exists. If what you want is for it to be more performant, that needs to be done by the implementors of it.
content.formatted_body: .json already exists. If what you want is for it to be more performant, that needs to be done by the implementors of it.

content.body: If what you want is a different thing, TC39, WhatWG, or WinterTC might each be reasonable, depending on the thing you want. If you have a use case for parsing binary json that isn't just "I want `.json` to be faster" then it might be reasonable to do it in TC39
content.formatted_body: If what you want is a different thing, TC39, WhatWG, or WinterTC might each be reasonable, depending on the thing you want. If you have a use case for parsing binary json that isn't just "I want .json to be faster" then it might be reasonable to do it in TC39

content.body:
But still thanks for this discussion. I literally spent previous week in solitude.
Making changes to existing APIs is much more likely to be avoided, as the changes are not backwards-compatible.
content.formatted_body:

content.body: `.json` could operate on raw bytes, without an intermediate string, with no user-visible changes
content.formatted_body: .json could operate on raw bytes, without an intermediate string, with no user-visible changes

content.body: So yes, for me it is reasonable to propose my ideas to TC39, because they are new, clearly express developers’ intentions, can be used in browser and on the backend, improve performance
content.formatted_body: So yes, for me it is reasonable to propose my ideas to TC39, because they are new, clearly express developers’ intentions, can be used in browser and on the backend, improve performance
